title: Risky business under Labor
full_text:
About 30 businesses have gone bust per day since Labor won government, with the construction, hospitality and retail industries the hardest hit.
The latest insolvency figures from corporate and financial services regulator ASIC show 14,011 businesses went under in the 2025-26 financial year.
They are among 47,728 businesses that have entered insolvency since May 2022, when Anthony Albanese beat Liberal prime minister Scott Morrison in the federal election.
Before Coalition government funding kept industries afloat during the Covid pandemic, 8105 businesses went bust in 2018-19, or 22 per day.
The construction sector has recorded the largest number of insolvencies since 2022, with 10,166, followed by accommodation and hospitality with 6356.
The figures come as small businesses groups prepare to oppose looming changes to discretionary trusts, with Council of Small Business Organisations Australia warning 210,000 small family businesses could face a higher tax burden once the changes come into effect from July 2028.
The government, however, says only eight per cent of small businesses will be affected.
Opposition Leader Angus Taylor will weaponise the insolvency statistics at a small business and tax forum in the outer northern Sydney suburb of Berowra today.
He is expected to tell about 200 guests the Coalition’s plan to fix the economy “starts with backing small businesses”.
“A Coalition government I lead will axe Labor’s toxic taxes on small businesses, bring down power bills by scrapping net zero, establish a permanent $50,000 instant asset write-off for businesses with a turnover under $10m, and expand access to CGT concessions to thousands of small businesses,” Mr Taylor is expected to say.
While business confidence improved last month according to a NAB survey released yesterday, NAB business group executive Andrew Auerbach said businesses were still facing “price pressures”.
“Businesses are looking at every part of their operation to find savings,” he said.
“Some are reducing fuel and transport costs by planning routes more efficiently or investing in more fuel-efficient site equipment, while others are working closely with suppliers and customers to manage costs and keep goods moving.” Berowra Liberal MP Julian Leeser said the budget had “taken a baseball bat to business confidence”, with 13 insolvencies recorded in the Hornsby local government area last month alone. “I’ve met business owners across Berowra who are considering their options, seeking advice from accountants and wondering whether they can continue,” he said.
